title: Chow Quotients of Toric and Schubert Varieties by ℂ∗-actions
speaker: Botong Wang
abstract:
Chow quotients of projective varieties by affine torus actions provide alternative constructions of interesting geometric objects. For example, the moduli space of stable genus 0 curves with n
 marked points arises as the Chow quotient of the Grassmannian Gr(2,n) by the n-torus, and the wonderful model of a hyperplane arrangement is the Chow quotient of the matroid Schubert variety by the ℂ∗-action. In this talk, we focus on the Chow quotients of projective varieties by ℂ∗-actions. For toric varieties, the work of Kapranov, Sturmfels and Zelevinsky shows that the Chow quotient is another toric variety associated with the fiber polytope. Extending this perspective, we demonstrate that, under certain conditions, the local geometry of the Chow quotient is governed by a toric variety. As an application, we establish rational smoothness criteria for Chow quotients of Schubert varieties by ℂ∗-actions. This is joint work with Mateusz Michalek and Leonid Monin.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Kernel framework and geometric motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls kernels on graded posets, their left and right Kazhdan-Lusztig-Stanley polynomials, and the newer Chow polynomial, using known Coxeter, polytopal, and matroid geometries to pose the problem of finding Chow-quotient geometry behind these combinatorial formulas.

proof_stages.label: Bialynicki-Birula data for C^*-actions
proof_stages.summary: For a projective variety with finite C^*-fixed locus the speaker defines attracting and repelling BB pieces, relates them to Schubert and opposite Schubert cells on flag varieties, and translates them for toric polytopes into minima and maxima of faces with respect to a linear functional.

proof_stages.label: Polytopal BB order and stratification criterion
proof_stages.summary: Under an irreducibility hypothesis on the closures of BB cells, the relation between vertices defined by common faces is shown to have equivalent formulations: BB-plus stratifies, BB-minus stratifies, and the relation is transitive, thereby producing the relevant graded poset.

proof_stages.label: Kernel and KLS polynomials from BB intersections
proof_stages.summary: With the stratification hypotheses and a suitable local simplicity condition, the face counts in intersections BB^+(u) ∩ BB^-(v) define a kernel on the vertex poset, and the associated left and right KLS polynomials are identified with toric g-polynomials of the corresponding vertex-star cones.

proof_stages.label: Chow quotient mechanism for orbit degenerations
proof_stages.summary: The Chow quotient of a projective C^*-variety is defined as the closure, in the Chow variety, of the family of orbit closures over the good quotient, so boundary points encode broken orbit cycles; toric examples recover the Kapranov-Zelevinsky permutohedral quotient and explain the wonderful compactification as a Chow quotient of a matroid Schubert variety.

proof_stages.label: Chow quotient realization of the Chow polynomial
proof_stages.summary: For Richardson and toric varieties the Chow quotient carries a stratification by the ways orbit curves break, and checking this stratification gives the desired Chow-polynomial formulas, filling the new row of the kernel/KLS/Chow table for the BB vertex poset.

proof_stages.label: Rationally smooth Chow and graph-space models
proof_stages.summary: The palindromicity of the Chow polynomial is explained by rational smoothness of Chow quotients of Richardson varieties, and a birational rationally smooth model of a Richardson variety is obtained from the Chow quotient of P^1 times the Richardson variety by intersecting the universal cycle with a horizontal slice; the stable-map version gives stronger smoothness statements.

examples.note: The matroid row, via arrangement complements, the reciprocal/matroid Schubert variety, and the wonderful model, is presented as a source of the Chow-polynomial motivation and later as an analogy for Chow quotients.
